what_it_does: Implements a single consolidated MCP tool named `node` that dispatches four operations—create, delete, propose, apply—by wrapping existing `Suite` methods, with per-op parameter validation and teaching error messages.
observations: The tool is registered via `register(mcp, suite)`, which decorates the inner `node` function with `@mcp.tool()` and returns it.
Four ops are grouped into one resource because all are writes on the `node` resource.
Parameter sets are disjoint: create/delete use `graph`/`node_id`; propose/apply use `name`/`spec`/`surfaced_id`.
Validation returns descriptive error dicts rather than raising exceptions.
Governance for `apply` is delegated to `Suite.apply_node`, which reads approval from the substrate; the wrapper has no `confirmed` parameter.
The docstring is extensive and explains each op plus governance constraints.
standouts: Consolidates four previously flat tools into one op-dispatched tool while explicitly preserving the existing governance floor.
The wrapper deliberately prevents self-approval by not exposing any `confirmed` parameter.
conventions: Follows MCP tool registration pattern with `@mcp.tool()` decorator.
Returns dict payloads for both success and error cases.
concerns: Default empty strings for required fields delay missing-argument detection until explicit validation.
The `config` default is a mutable dict literal in the signature, though it is copied with `dict(config)` before use.
Error messages are long inline strings, which may complicate testing or localization.
No type hints on `mcp` or `suite` parameters.
notes: A consolidated, governance-preserving wrapper around Suite node operations with built-in parameter teaching.
questions: What exact methods does `suite` expose and what do their return dicts contain?
How does `Suite.apply_node` resolve and enforce the operator approval check?
What is the schema/contract for the `mcp.tool()` decorator?
Are other tools in this codebase consolidated in the same pattern?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose—consolidate four node tools into one op-dispatched tool while keeping governance inside Suite—matches the implementation.
apparent_themes: Agent-teaching through docstrings and error messages
intention_signals: The file is trying to reduce the surface area of node-related MCP tools from four separate endpoints to one op-selector endpoint without changing trust boundaries, so agents still route through the same Suite governance but get clearer guidance on which parameters belong to which operation.
novelty: The deliberate refusal to expose a `confirmed` flag on `apply`, forcing approval to be read from the substrate rather than the caller, is a notable trust-boundary design choice.
contribution: Provides a single, self-documenting `node` tool entry point that validates inputs and dispatches to existing Suite node lifecycle methods, preserving governance while reducing the number of tools an agent must discover.
summary_for_embedding: A consolidated MCP tool wrapper that dispatches create/delete/propose/apply node operations to existing Suite methods with parameter validation and governance-preserving restrictions.
intention_for_embedding: To collapse four flat node tools into one op-dispatched tool while keeping all governance and approval logic inside the Suite layer.

symbols.does: Factory that registers the consolidated `node` MCP tool with the given `mcp` server and `suite` instance.
symbols.description: Accepts `mcp` and `suite`, applies the `@mcp.tool()` decorator to the inner `node` function, and returns it. It contains no business logic beyond registration setup.

symbols.does: Dispatches one of four node operations—create, delete, propose, apply—to the corresponding `Suite` method after validating the required parameters for each op.
symbols.description: Implements the consolidated node tool: validates `op`, returns teaching errors for missing fields, calls `suite.create_node`, `suite.delete_node`, `suite.propose_node`, or `suite.apply_node` as appropriate, and returns structured dict results. It deliberately does not expose an approval flag, leaving governance to the Suite.
